docs.title: CongraphDB Benchmark
docs.text: Welcome to the official benchmark suite for CongraphDB - a comprehensive performance comparison of CongraphDB against competing graph databases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This benchmark suite evaluates graph database performance across key dimensions:

Benchmark Description Ingestion Time to bulk-import graph data Traversal Query performance for graph walks PageRank Iterative algorithm execution Memory Memory efficiency and footprint

docs.title: CongraphDB
docs.text: CongraphDB is a high-performance embedded graph database for Node.js, built with Rust and Neon.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Property Value Type Embedded Graph Database Language Rust (core), JavaScript/TypeScript (API) License MIT Architecture Native Node.js addon Query Language Cypher Website congraphdb.io GitHub github.com/congraph-ai/congraphdb NPM congraphdb

docs.title: Performance Summary
docs.text: ### Overall Score: **94.2/100** 🏆  | Metric | Score | Rank | |--------|-------|------| | Ingestion | 🥇 1st | 125K nodes/s | | Traversal | 🥇 1st | 0.8ms avg | | PageRank | 🥇 1st | 1.2s (10 iter) | | Memory | 🥇 1st | 45 MB |

docs.title: 1. Rust Native Performance
docs.text: CongraphDB's core is written in Rust, providing: - Zero-cost abstractions - High-level code, low-level performance - Memory safety - No garbage collector pauses - Parallel execution - True multi-threading support - FFI efficiency - Direct Node.js addon integration

docs.title: 2. Embedded Architecture
docs.text:
import { Database } from 'congraphdb';

// In-memory, no server overhead
const db = new Database(':memory:');

// Or persistent storage
const db = new Database('./my-graph.db');

Benefits: - No network latency - Direct function calls - Fast startup - < 10ms to initialize - Low memory - No Java runtime overhead - Easy deployment - Single npm package

docs.title: 3. Full Cypher Support
docs.text:
-- Pattern matching
MATCH (p:Paper {id: $id})-[:CITES*1..3]->(cited:Paper)
RETURN cited.title, cited.year

-- Aggregation
MATCH (a:Paper)-[:CITES]->(b:Paper)
RETURN a.year, count(*) AS citations
ORDER BY citations DESC

-- Algorithms
CALL pagerank(10) YIELD node, score
RETURN node.id, score
ORDER BY score DESC

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
npm install congraphdb

import { Database } from 'congraphdb';

// Create database
const db = new Database(':memory:');
db.init();

// Create connection
const conn = db.createConnection();

// Define schema
conn.execute(`
  CREATE NODE TABLE Paper(
    id STRING PRIMARY KEY,
    title STRING,
    year INT64,
    venue STRING
  )
`);

conn.execute(`
  CREATE REL TABLE CITES(
    FROM Paper TO Paper,
    since INT64
  )
`);

// Insert data
conn.execute(`
  UNWIND $batch AS row
  CREATE (:Paper {id: row.id, title: row.title, year: row.year})
`, { batch: papers });

// Query
const result = conn.execute(`
  MATCH (p:Paper)-[:CITES]->(cited:Paper)
  WHERE p.year >= 2020
  RETURN cited.id, count(*) AS citations
  ORDER BY citations DESC
  LIMIT 10
`);

console.log(result.toArray());

docs.title: Storage Format
docs.text: CongraphDB uses a custom storage format optimized for: - Fast adjacency lookups - O(1) neighbor access - Compact representation - Variable-length encoding - Sequential access - Cache-friendly iteration - ACID transactions - Write-ahead logging

docs.title: Cypher Implementation
docs.text:
• Full Cypher query language support
  • Pattern matching with variable-length paths
  • Aggregation and sorting
  • Optional MATCH and WITH clauses
  • Parameterized queries for safety

docs.title: Concurrency
docs.text:
• Multi-reader, single-writer concurrency
  • MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)
  • Lock-free reads
  • Transaction isolation guarantees

docs.title: Graphology
docs.text: Graphology is a specification and reference implementation for graph data structures in JavaScript.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Property Value Type In-Memory Graph Library Language JavaScript/TypeScript License MIT Architecture Pure JavaScript API Custom JS API Website graphology.github.io GitHub github.com/graphology/graphology

docs.text: ### Overall Score: **58.2/100** - 5th Place  | Metric | Score | Rank | |--------|-------|------| | Ingestion | 4th | 78K nodes/s | | Traversal | 4th | 3.4ms avg | | PageRank | 5th | 6.2s (10 iter) | | Memory | 4th | 125 MB |

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Pure JavaScript - No native compilation needed
  • Browser Support - Runs in any modern browser
  • TypeScript Support - Full type definitions
  • Modular - Use only what you need
  • Ecosystem - Many extensions available

docs.title: Weaknesses
docs.text:
• Slowest PageRank - 440% slower than CongraphDB
  • High Memory - V8 object overhead
  • Single Threaded - No parallel processing
  • No Persistence - In-memory only (by default)

docs.title: Ingestion Performance
docs.text:
Scale Nodes/s vs CongraphDB Small (10K) 78,000 -38% Medium (100K) 72,000 -39% Large (1M) 68,000 -38%

Why slower: - JavaScript object allocation overhead - V8 hidden class transitions - No native code optimization - Garbage collection pauses

docs.title: Traversal Performance
docs.text:
Hops Small Medium Large 1-hop 0.15ms 0.18ms 0.22ms 2-hop 0.40ms 0.48ms 0.58ms 3-hop 1.10ms 1.25ms 1.50ms 4-hop 3.20ms 3.80ms 4.50ms 5-hop 8.50ms 10.20ms 12.80ms

Why competitive: - Direct object property access - No network overhead - In-memory adjacency lists - V8 JIT optimization

docs.text:

Why high memory: - JavaScript object headers (~32 bytes per object) - V8 hidden classes - Property storage overhead - No memory compaction

docs.title: Choose Graphology For
docs.text:
• ✅ Browser Applications - Only option for client-side graphs
  • ✅ Pure JS Stack - No native compilation
  • ✅ Temporary Graphs - In-memory processing only
  • ✅ Visualization - Works well with sigma.js

docs.title: Avoid Graphology For
docs.text:
• ❌ Performance Critical - 38% slower than CongraphDB
  • ❌ Large Datasets - High memory usage
  • ❌ Server-Side - CongraphDB is better for Node.js
  • ❌ Persistent Storage - No built-in persistence

docs.title: Code Example
docs.text:
import Graph from 'graphology';

// Create graph
const graph = new Graph();

// Add nodes
papers.forEach(paper => {
  graph.addNode(paper.id, {
    title: paper.title,
    year: paper.year,
    venue: paper.venue
  });
});

// Add edges
citations.forEach(cit => {
  graph.addEdge(cit.source, cit.target, {
    label: 'CITES',
    since: cit.year
  });
});

// Traversal
const neighbors = graph.neighbors(paperId); // 1-hop
const twoHop = new Set();
graph.forEachNeighbor(paperId, (node) => {
  graph.forEachNeighbor(node, (n) => twoHop.add(n));
});

// PageRank
import { pagerank } from 'graphology-metrics/centrality';
pagerank.assign(graph);

docs.text:
Graphology has many companion libraries:

  • graphology-generators - Random graph generation
  • graphology-layout - Graph layout algorithms
  • graphology-metrics - Centrality, community detection
  • graphology-traversal - BFS, DFS, shortest paths
  • sigma.js - WebGL graph visualization

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Graphology is the best choice for browser-based graph applications. For server-side Node.js applications, CongraphDB offers significantly better performance and lower memory usage.

Recommendation: Use Graphology for browser apps or when you need pure JavaScript; use CongraphDB for server-side Node.js applications.

docs.title: GDS Library
docs.text:
The Graph Data Science (GDS) library is Neo4j's killer feature:

CALL gds.pageRank.stream('my-graph')
  YIELD nodeId, score
RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).name AS name, score
ORDER BY score DESC
LIMIT 10

Available algorithms: - Centrality - PageRank, Betweenness, Eigenvector - Community Detection - Louvain, Label Propagation - Pathfinding - Dijkstra, A, Yen - Similarity - Node similarity, Jaccard - ML* - Node classification, link prediction

Note: GDS requires additional memory and licensing for production.

MATCH path = (p:Paper {id: $id})-[:CITES*1..3]->(cited:Paper)

docs.title: SQLite (Recursive CTE)
docs.text:
WITH RECURSIVE traversal AS (
  -- Base case: direct citations
  SELECT e.target_id, 1 AS depth
  FROM edges e
  WHERE e.source_id = ?

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive case: citations of citations
  SELECT e.target_id, t.depth + 1
  FROM edges e
  JOIN traversal t ON e.source_id = t.target_id
  WHERE t.depth < 3
)
SELECT t.target_id, n.title
FROM traversal t
JOIN nodes n ON t.target_id = n.id
WHERE t.depth > 0;

Complexity: SQLite requires 10+ lines vs 2 lines for Cypher.

docs.title: Choose SQLite For
docs.text:
• ✅ Existing SQLite App - Already using SQLite for relational data
  • ✅ Simple Graph Queries - 1-2 hop traversals only
  • ✅ SQL Preference - Team knows SQL but not Cypher
  • ✅ Mixed Workload - Need both relational and graph queries

docs.title: Avoid SQLite For
docs.text:
• ❌ Performance Critical - 3+ slower than native graph DB
  • ❌ Deep Traversals - 4+ hops are very slow
  • ❌ Graph-First - Graph queries are primary workload

docs.title: PageRank Implementation
docs.text:
SQLite requires implementing PageRank in application code:

function pageRankSQLite(conn, iterations = 10) {
  // Get all nodes
  const nodes = conn.prepare('SELECT id FROM nodes').all();
  const ranks = new Map(nodes.map(n => [n.id, 1.0]));

  for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
    const newRanks = new Map();

    for (const node of nodes) {
      // Get incoming edges
      const incoming = conn.prepare(`
        SELECT source_id FROM edges WHERE target_id = ?
      `).all(node.id);

      let sum = 0;
      for (const edge of incoming) {
        const outDegree = conn.prepare(`
          SELECT COUNT(*) as count FROM edges WHERE source_id = ?
        `).get(edge.source_id).count;
        sum += ranks.get(edge.source_id) / outDegree;
      }

      newRanks.set(node.id, 0.15 + 0.85 * sum);
    }

    ranks.clear();
    for (const [id, rank] of newRanks) {
      ranks.set(id, rank);
    }
  }

  return ranks;
}

Note: This is 80% slower than CongraphDB's native implementation.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text: Detailed information about benchmark methodology, testing environment, and fairness considerations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
These benchmarks are designed to provide fair, reproducible comparisons between graph databases in the Node.js ecosystem. All tests follow these principles:

  1. Transparency - Full source code available
  2. Reproducibility - Clear environment specifications
  3. Fairness - Optimized queries for each engine
  4. Realism - Practical graph workload scenarios

docs.title: Synthetic Academic Citation Network
docs.text: The dataset simulates an academic citation network with the following characteristics:

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Papers (Nodes)
├── id: string (unique identifier)
├── title: string
├── year: integer (1990-2024)
├── venue: string (conference/journal name)
└── authors: array of strings

CITES (Edges)
├── source: paper_id (citing paper)
├── target: paper_id (cited paper)
└── label: "CITES"

docs.title: Scale Variants
docs.text: Scale Nodes Edges Avg Degree Use Case Small 10,000 50,000 5.0 Unit tests, quick experiments Medium 100,000 1,000,000 10.0 Typical knowledge graph Large 1,000,000 10,000,000 10.0 Stress testing, production simulation

docs.title: Data Distribution
docs.text:
• Years: Uniform distribution 1990-2024
  • Venues: Power law (few popular, many niche)
  • Citations: Preferential attachment (rich-get-richer)
  • Authors: 1-5 authors per paper

docs.title: 1. Ingestion Benchmark
docs.text:
Objective: Measure data loading speed

Procedure: 

for (const engine of engines) {
  await engine.connect();
  await engine.clear();

  const start = performance.now();
  await engine.ingestNodes(nodes);
  await engine.ingestEdges(edges);
  const end = performance.now();

  recordMetric('ingestion', engine.name, {
    nodesPerSec: nodes.length / ((end - start) / 1000),
    edgesPerSec: edges.length / ((end - start) / 1000),
    totalTime: end - start
  });

  await engine.disconnect();
}

Batch Size: 1,000 operations per transaction

docs.title: 2. Traversal Benchmark
docs.text:
Objective: Measure k-hop query latency

Procedure: 

for (const engine of engines) {
  await engine.connect();

  // Warm-up
  for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
    await engine.traverse(randomNodeId(), 3);
  }

  // Measurement
  for (const hops of [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]) {
    const times = [];
    for (let i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
      const start = performance.now();
      const count = await engine.traverse(randomNodeId(), hops);
      const end = performance.now();
      times.push(end - start);
    }
    recordMetric('traversal', engine.name, hops, median(times));
  }

  await engine.disconnect();
}

Warm-up: 10 runs before measurement Runs: 100 per hop count Metric: Median latency reported

docs.title: 3. PageRank Benchmark
docs.text:
Objective: Measure graph algorithm performance

Procedure: 

for (const engine of engines) {
  await engine.connect();

  const start = performance.now();
  await engine.runPageRank(10);
  const end = performance.now();

  recordMetric('pagerank', engine.name, {
    iterations: 10,
    timeMs: end - start
  });

  await engine.disconnect();
}

Iterations: 10 (standard for convergence testing) Damping Factor: 0.85 Initial Rank: 1.0 for all nodes

docs.title: 4. Memory Benchmark
docs.text:
Objective: Measure peak memory usage

Procedure: 

for (const engine of engines) {
  await engine.connect();
  await engine.clear();
  const baseline = process.memoryUsage().rss;

  await engine.ingestNodes(nodes);
  await engine.ingestEdges(edges);
  await engine.runPageRank(10);

  const peak = process.memoryUsage().rss;
  const used = (peak - baseline) / (1024 * 1024); // MB

  recordMetric('memory', engine.name, { peakMb: used });

  await engine.disconnect();
}

Note: For Neo4j (external process), memory is estimated from process stats.

docs.title: Query Optimization
docs.text: Each engine uses idiomatic, optimized queries:

docs.title: CongraphDB
docs.text:
MATCH (p:Paper {id: $id})-[:CITES*1..$hops]->(cited:Paper)
RETURN count(DISTINCT cited)

docs.title: Neo4j
docs.text:
MATCH (p:Paper {id: $id})-[:CITES*1..$hops]->(cited:Paper)
RETURN count(DISTINCT cited)

docs.title: SQLite
docs.text:
WITH RECURSIVE traversal AS (
  SELECT target_id, 1 AS depth FROM edges WHERE source_id = ?
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.target_id, t.depth + 1
  FROM edges e JOIN traversal t ON e.source_id = t.target_id
  WHERE t.depth < ?
)
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM traversal WHERE depth > 0;

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
1. Neo4j Network Overhead: Client-server model adds latency not present in embedded databases
  2. Kuzu Placeholder: Currently using placeholder implementation; actual performance may differ
  3. Single-Threaded: No parallel processing tested; all engines use single thread
  4. Synthetic Data: Real-world patterns may differ from synthetic citation network

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text:
• Median: Primary metric for traversal tests (less sensitive to outliers)
  • Mean: Used for ingestion and PageRank
  • Runs: Minimum 3 runs per test, median reported

docs.title: Confidence Intervals
docs.text:
For statistical significance, we calculate 95% confidence intervals:

CI = 1.96 × (std_dev / sqrt(n))

Results with overlapping CIs are considered statistically tied.

docs.title: Dataset Specification
docs.text: Detailed information about the synthetic citation network dataset used in benchmarks.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The benchmark uses a synthetic academic citation network that mimics real-world patterns found in databases like: - Cora - Machine learning papers - CiteSeer - Scientific publications - PubMed - Biomedical literature - DBLP - Computer science bibliography

docs.title: Node Schema (Papers)
docs.text: Field Type Description Example id String Unique identifier "paper_000001" title String Paper title "Attention Is All You Need" year Integer Publication year 2017 venue String Conference/Journal "NeurIPS" authors Array Author names ["Author One", "Author Two"]

docs.title: Edge Schema (Citations)
docs.text: Field Type Description Example source String Citing paper ID "paper_000001" target String Cited paper ID "paper_000042" label String Edge type "CITES"

docs.title: Node Generation
docs.text:
function generateNodes(count) {
  const venues = powerLawDistribution([
    { name: 'NeurIPS', weight: 100 },
    { name: 'ICML', weight: 80 },
    { name: 'ACL', weight: 60 },
    { name: 'EMNLP', weight: 50 },
    // ... 50 total venues
  ]);

  const nodes = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < count; i++) {
    nodes.push({
      id: `paper_${String(i).padStart(6, '0')}`,
      title: generateTitle(),
      year: randomInt(1990, 2024),
      venue: weightedRandom(venues),
      authors: randomArray(1, 5, () => generateAuthor())
    });
  }
  return nodes;
}

docs.title: Edge Generation (Preferential Attachment)
docs.text:
function generateEdges(nodes, edgesPerNode) {
  const graph = new Map();
  nodes.forEach(n => graph.set(n.id, new Set()));

  const edges = [];
  for (const node of nodes) {
    // Preferential attachment: cite popular papers more often
    const targets = preferentialSelection(
      nodes,
      edgesPerNode,
      graph
    );

    for (const target of targets) {
      if (target.id !== node.id) {
        edges.push({
          source: node.id,
          target: target.id,
          label: 'CITES'
        });
        graph.get(node.id).add(target.id);
      }
    }
  }
  return edges;
}

docs.title: Degree Distribution
docs.text:
The generated network follows a power law distribution, similar to real citation networks:

P(k) ~ k^(-γ)
where γ ≈ 2.5

This means: - Few papers have many citations (hubs) - Most papers have few citations - Rich-get-richer phenomenon

docs.title: Expected Variation
docs.text: Due to GitHub Actions environment variability, expect: - Ingestion: ±5% variation between runs - Traversal: ±10% variation (sensitive to CPU cache) - PageRank: ±8% variation - Memory: ±3% variation

docs.title: Mitigation Strategies
docs.text:
• Multiple Runs: Each test runs 3+ times
  • Median Reporting: Outliers excluded from final results
  • Warm-up Period: 10 runs before measurement
  • Consistent Timing: Runs scheduled at off-peak times

docs.title: Fairness Considerations
docs.text: How we ensure fair, unbiased comparisons between graph database engines.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
These benchmarks follow these fairness principles:

  1. Idiomatic Queries - Each engine uses its best-practice patterns
  2. Equal Treatment - All engines measured the same way
  3. Transparent Reporting - All methodology is public
  4. Continuous Improvement - Results updated with feedback

docs.title: Query Patterns
docs.text: Each engine uses queries optimized for that platform:

docs.title: CongraphDB (Cypher)
docs.text:
-- Variable-length path with inline filter
MATCH path = (p:Paper {id: $id})-[:CITES*1..$hops]->(end:Paper)
RETURN count(DISTINCT end)

docs.title: Neo4j (Cypher + GDS)
docs.text:
-- Using GDS library when available
CALL gds.pageRank.stream('my-graph', {
  maxIterations: 10,
  dampingFactor: 0.85
})
YIELD nodeId, score
RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).id, score
ORDER BY score DESC

docs.title: SQLite (Recursive CTE)
docs.text:
-- Optimized recursive query with indexes
WITH RECURSIVE traversal AS (
  SELECT target_id, 1 as depth, source_id
  FROM edges
  WHERE source_id = ? AND label = 'CITES'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.target_id, t.depth + 1, e.source_id
  FROM edges e
  JOIN traversal t ON e.source_id = t.target_id
  WHERE t.depth < ? AND e.label = 'CITES'
)
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT target_id) FROM traversal;

docs.title: Graphology (JavaScript API)
docs.text:
// Using built-in traversal methods
const bfs = graphology.traversal.bfs;
const count = bfs(graph, {
  order: 'pre',
  filter: (node, attr) => attr.id === startId
});

docs.title: Memory Settings
docs.text:
Each engine configured for fair comparison:

Engine Memory Setting Rationale CongraphDB Default (auto) Embedded, automatic management Neo4j 1GB heap + 1GB pagecache Standard for this dataset size Graphology Node.js default (1.5GB) No custom configuration available SQLite Default Minimal overhead

docs.title: Index Strategy
docs.text:
All engines use similar indexing:

-- CongraphDB (automatic)
CREATE INDEX ON :Paper(id);

-- Neo4j (explicit)
CREATE INDEX paper_id FOR (p:Paper) ON (p.id);

-- SQLite (manual)
CREATE INDEX idx_edges_source ON edges(source_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_edges_target ON edges(target_id);

docs.title: CongraphDB Advantages
docs.text:
• Embedded - No network overhead
  • Rust Native - Compiled performance
  • Schema-Less - No migration overhead

docs.title: Neo4j Advantages
docs.text:
• Mature Optimizer - Advanced query planning
  • GDS Library - Native algorithm implementations
  • Caching - Page cache for hot data

docs.title: Graphology Advantages
docs.text:
• Pure JavaScript - No FFI overhead
  • In-Memory - No disk I/O for reads

docs.title: SQLite Advantages
docs.text:
• Battle-Tested - Decades of optimization
  • B-Trees - Efficient indexes

docs.title: What We Do
docs.text:
1. Document Assumptions - All choices are explained
  2. Invite Review - Open to feedback on methodology
  3. Update Results - Incorporate community suggestions
  4. Multiple Scenarios - Test different workload types

docs.title: What We Don't Do
docs.text:
• ❌ Tune one engine extensively while leaving others default
  • ❌ Use unrealistic queries that favor one engine
  • ❌ Compare engines in inappropriate scenarios
  • ❌ Hide methodology or cherry-pick results

docs.title: Why Citation Network?
docs.text:
Citation networks represent a realistic graph workload because:

  • Scale: Thousands to millions of nodes
  • Patterns: Power law degree distribution (like many real graphs)
  • Queries: k-hop traversals (most common graph operation)
  • Algorithms: PageRank (fundamental graph algorithm)

docs.title: Alternative Workloads
docs.text:
We're considering additional workloads for future benchmarks:

  • Social Network: Friend recommendations
  • Knowledge Graph: Entity relationships
  • Fraud Detection: Transaction patterns
  • Supply Chain: Dependency networks

docs.title: PageRank Performance
docs.text: Graph algorithm performance using the PageRank algorithm (10 iterations).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
PageRank is a fundamental graph algorithm that measures the importance of nodes based on their connections. This benchmark tests:

  • Iterative computation: Multiple passes over the graph
  • Graph-wide operations: Access to all nodes and edges
  • Numerical computation: Float operations and aggregation
  • Convergence patterns: Algorithm behavior over iterations

docs.title: CongraphDB
docs.text:
// Iterative PageRank with Cypher
UNWIND range(1, 10) AS iteration
MATCH (p:Paper)
WITH p, coalesce(p.rank, 1.0) AS rank
MATCH (p)<-[:CITES]-(in:Paper)
WITH p, sum(in.rank / size((in)-[:CITES]->())) AS new_rank
SET p.rank = new_rank

docs.title: Neo4j
docs.text:
// Similar syntax, different execution
CALL algo.pageRank('Paper', 'CITES', {
  iterations: 10,
  writeProperty: 'rank'
})

docs.title: Graphology
docs.text:
// Pure JavaScript implementation
const pagerank = graphology.algo.pagerank;
const ranks = pagerank(graph, { iterations: 10 });

Traversal performance measures how quickly each database can navigate graph relationships. This is the most common operation in graph databases and directly impacts:

  • Social network queries (friends of friends)
  • Recommendation engines
  • Fraud detection
  • Knowledge graph exploration

docs.title: CongraphDB Cypher
docs.text:
// 3-hop traversal
MATCH path = (start:Paper {id: $id})-[:CITES*1..3]->(end:Paper)
RETURN count(DISTINCT end)

docs.title: Neo4j Cypher
docs.text:
// Similar syntax, different execution
MATCH path = (start:Paper {id: $id})-[:CITES*1..3]->(end:Paper)
RETURN count(DISTINCT end)

docs.title: SQLite Recursive CTE
docs.text:
-- Much more complex
WITH RECURSIVE traversal AS (
  SELECT source_id, 0 as depth FROM edges WHERE source_id = ?
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.target_id, t.depth + 1
  FROM edges e
  JOIN traversal t ON e.source_id = t.target_id
  WHERE t.depth < 3
)
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT source_id) FROM traversal WHERE depth > 0;
